name: Partnership — Sharing Profit Fairly
whatIsIt: When people run a business together, profit is split in the ratio of what each one actually 'put in' — and that means money AND time. Each partner's share weight = capital × months invested. Divide the profit in the ratio of these weights. If everyone invests for the same time, the ratio is simply the ratio of their capitals.
whyNeeded: Family businesses, start-ups, chit funds, and shop partnerships all split earnings this way. Partnership is a steady favourite in SSC, Bank and Railway aptitude papers, and the capital × time idea keeps the split fair when partners join or leave at different times.
realLifeUses: Two friends opening a tea stall with different amounts of money
A start-up where founders join at different months
Splitting rent or trip costs by how long each person stayed
A chit fund or investment pool sharing returns
SSC / Bank / Railway quantitative aptitude questions

vocabulary.meaning: The money a partner invests in the business.
Each partner's share weight — money multiplied by the months it stayed invested.
Profit is divided in the ratio of the partners' capital × time weights.
A working partner may take an extra salary before profit is split by capital.

story.title: The Two-Friend Tea Stall
story.text: Kiran puts ₹5,000 into a tea stall; Meena puts ₹3,000. At year end they make ₹4,000 profit. 'Half each?' asks Kiran. Meena's father shakes his head: 'Share it by what each put in. Both invested the whole year, so the ratio is just the money: 5000 : 3000 = 5 : 3. That's 8 equal shares. Kiran gets 5 shares, Meena 3. Each share is 4000 ÷ 8 = ₹500, so Kiran gets ₹2,500 and Meena ₹1,500.' Fair and clear.
story.extractedProblem: Kiran invests ₹5000, Meena ₹3000, both for the full year; profit ₹4000. What is Kiran's share?
story.answerInStory: ₹2,500 — ratio 5 : 3, eight shares of ₹500, Kiran takes five.

visual.caption: Profit follows the ratio of capital × time. Same time → ratio is just the money. Total parts → value per part → each partner's cut.

teachingGallery.title: Split by capital × time
teachingGallery.note: Weight = capital × months. Add the weights for total parts; profit ÷ total parts = one part's value.

examples.caption: 12000 : 12000 = 1 : 1 — different money & time can still be equal!

standardMethod.summary: For each partner compute capital × months; make the ratio; split profit by that ratio.
steps: For each partner, weight = capital × time (in the same time unit, e.g. months).
Write the ratio of weights and simplify.
Total parts = sum of the ratio numbers; one part = profit ÷ total parts.
Each partner's share = their ratio number × one part.

alternateMethods.name: Equal-time shortcut
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When all partners invest for the same duration.
steps: Time cancels out — just use the ratio of capitals.
Split profit in the capital ratio.

workedExamples.problem: A invests ₹6000 and B invests ₹4000 for the same time. Profit ₹2500. Find B's share.
A invests ₹1000 for 12 months; B invests ₹2000 for 6 months. Profit ₹3000. Find A's share.
steps: A's share = 1 × 1500 = ₹1500 (equal split, even though amounts differ).

trickPractice.intro: Multiply each partner's money by their months, ratio them, then split.

questions.q: A:₹2000, B:₹2000, same time. Profit ₹800. A's share (₹)?
A:₹3000, B:₹1000, same time. Profit ₹800. A's share (₹)?
A:₹5000, B:₹5000, same time. Profit ₹1000. B's share (₹)?

commonMistakes.mistake: Splitting only by money when partners invested for different lengths of time.
commonMistakes.fix: Profit follows capital × TIME. Someone who invests less money but for much longer can still earn an equal (or bigger) share. Always multiply money by months first.

commonMistakes.mistake: Dividing profit equally regardless of how much each partner invested.
commonMistakes.fix: Equal split only happens when the capital × time weights are equal. Otherwise use the ratio of the weights.

easy.q: A:₹4000, B:₹2000, same time. Profit ₹600. A's share (₹)?
Two partners invest equal money for equal time. Profit is split…

medium.q: A:₹8000, B:₹12000, same time. Profit ₹1000. B's share (₹)?

challenge.q: A puts in less money but stays invested twice as long as B. It's possible A's share is…

options.label: Equal to or more than B's

challenge.answer: Equal to or more than B's
hintLadder: Extra time can balance less money.
So A can match or beat B.

questions.q: A:₹1000, B:₹1000, same time. Profit ₹500. A's share (₹)?
A:₹6000, B:₹2000, same time. Profit ₹800. A's share (₹)?
A partner's profit share depends on…

options.label: Only how much money, never time

questions.q: A:₹9000, B:₹3000, same time. Profit ₹1600. B's share (₹)?

teachBackPrompt: Teach a friend the partnership rule: profit is split by capital × time. Use A ₹1000 for 12 months and B ₹2000 for 6 months, show both weights are 12000, and explain why they split the profit equally despite investing different amounts.

revisionCard.summary: Profit ratio = ratio of (capital × time) weights. Same time → just compare money. Total parts → one part = profit ÷ total → each share = ratio number × one part. More time can offset less money. Equal split only when weights are equal.

gameMission.title: Fair Shares Stall
gameMission.brief: Run the stall, weigh each partner's capital × time, and split the day's profit so nobody grumbles. Catch the traps where time changes everything!

realLifeProject: Plan a pretend two-person business: each 'invests' a made-up amount for a made-up number of months. Compute the capital × time weights and split a pretend profit fairly — then swap the times and watch the fair split change.

rememberIt.unpack: Weight each partner by money × months. Ratio the weights. Same time → just the money.

studentQuestions.a: Because ₹1000 kept in for a whole year does more 'work' than ₹1000 kept in for one month — time matters as much as money.

studentQuestions.q: What if times are the same?
studentQuestions.a: Then time cancels out and you just split in the ratio of the amounts invested.
